coredata.dc:title: Two of a kind but different: Luminescent carbon quantum dots from Citrus peels for iron and tartrazine sensing and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Chatzimitakos, Theodoros
Kasouni, Athanasia
Sygellou, Lamprini
Avgeropoulos, Apostolos
Troganis, Anastasios
Stalikas, Constantine
coredata.dc:description: Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon peels were used to synthesize two different kinds of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via an unsophisticated and inexpensive carbonization procedure. The proposed synthesis is straightforward and adheres to the principles of green chemistry since no organic solvents are used and no toxic by-products are formed, while the residual resources employed facilitate the large scale synthesis of dots. The Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon peels are proved to be excellent precursors for the synthesis of CQDs with highly practical applications. The CQDs display strong excitation-independent, blue fluorescence, which is stable over time. Splendid water dispersibility, photostability and stability over a wide range of pH are some of the main advantages of the CQDs, which enable them to be used as a fluorescent probes. Although many of their features are alike, our findings demonstrate that each kind of the CQDs lend itself to quite distinct analytical applications. The developed fluorescent probes possess high potential for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ (Citrus sinensis CQDs) and tartrazine (Citrus limon CQDs) via a quenching mechanism. The decrease in fluorescence intensity is in linear relationship with the concentrations of Fe3+ and tartrazine in the ranges of 0.01–1.0μM and 0.6–23.5μΜ, respectively. Moreover, their low cytotoxicity reinforces their applicability towards cell bioimaging and intracellular detection of Fe3+, which were further studied.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon peels
Green synthesis
Photoluminescence
Applications
Quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from orange and lemon peels
Sensing approaches for Fe3+ and tartrazine determination
Intracellular detection of Fe3+ in HeLa cells
Optimization for Fe3+ and tartrazine sensing
Intracellular detection of Fe3+ in HeLa cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Deconvoluted XPS peaks of C1s, O1s and N1s of (i) Citrus sinensis CQDs and (ii) Citrus limon CQDs.
Emission spectra of (A) Citrus sinensis and (B) Citrus limon CQDs at various excitation wavelengths.
Plot of fluorescence quenching versus Fe3+ concentration. Inset: Linear calibration curve in the low-concentration range.
Plot of fluorescence quenching versus tartrazine concentration. Inset: Linear calibration curve.
Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of (A) Fe3+ – Citrus sinensis CQDs and (B) tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs.
Fluorescence images (magnification 60×) of HEK-293 cells treated with (A) Citrus sinensis (B) and Citrus limon CQDs.

Percent of sp 2, sp 3, C–O and C˭O bonds as derived from C1s peak fitting, % pyridine-like (N˭C(H)) and amine/amide or pyrolic (N–C(H)) from N1s peak fitting and % atomic concentration of carbon, oxygen, potassium and nitrogen.

head.ce:title: Two of a kind but different: Luminescent carbon quantum dots from Citrus peels for iron and tartrazine sensing and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Biophysical Chemistry, Department of Biological Applications and Technologies, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Biophysical Chemistry, Department of Biological Applications and Technologies, University of Ioannina

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas/Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (FORTH/ICE-HT), Stadiou Str., P.O. Box 1414, GR – 26504 Rio-Patras, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas/Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (FORTH/ICE-HT)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science Engineering, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science Engineering, University of Ioannina

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon peels were used to synthesize two different kinds of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via an unsophisticated and inexpensive carbonization procedure. The proposed synthesis is straightforward and adheres to the principles of green chemistry since no organic solvents are used and no toxic by-products are formed, while the residual resources employed facilitate the large scale synthesis of dots. The Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon peels are proved to be excellent precursors for the synthesis of CQDs with highly practical applications. The CQDs display strong excitation-independent, blue fluorescence, which is stable over time. Splendid water dispersibility, photostability and stability over a wide range of pH are some of the main advantages of the CQDs, which enable them to be used as a fluorescent probes. Although many of their features are alike, our findings demonstrate that each kind of the CQDs lend itself to quite distinct analytical applications. The developed fluorescent probes possess high potential for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ (Citrus sinensis CQDs) and tartrazine (Citrus limon CQDs) via a quenching mechanism. The decrease in fluorescence intensity is in linear relationship with the concentrations of Fe3+ and tartrazine in the ranges of 0.01–1.0μM and 0.6–23.5μΜ, respectively. Moreover, their low cytotoxicity reinforces their applicability towards cell bioimaging and intracellular detection of Fe3+, which were further studied.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon peels

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials functioning alternatively to semiconductor quantum dots. In general, CQDs are quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanomaterials, typically smaller than 10nm, dominated by the presence of sp 2 carbon, oxygen-containing groups and doping elements [1,2]. Their splendid photostability, high quantum yield, versatile surface chemistry and low toxicity, accompanied by favorable biocompatibility, made feasible their use as cellular imaging agents or drug carriers as well as for fluorescence applications [1,3].

Although these fluorescent carbon-based sensors hold great promise in chemical and biological sensing applications, most of the procedures for their production employed so far, include severe synthetic conditions, toxic reagents and complex processes (e.g. acidic oxidation, combustion, electrochemical oxidation and laser irradiation), giving rise to concerns about their environmental impact [4–7]. In addition, the overall cost of these processes is further heightened by the organic substances, which are utilized as precursors [8]. Therefore, despite the novelty of these processes, frequently, the drawbacks counterbalance their applicability. It is desirable, therefore, to simplify the synthetic processes, so as to produce CQDs via environmentally benign, smart, low-cost procedures [9]. Recently, a few studies demonstrated the use of various natural sources (e.g. honey, milk, ground coffee, water chestnut) for the synthesis of CQDs [10–13]. Despite the advancement made in the selection of “green” carbon sources, the use of edible products is still limited. A more favorable alternative option is the re-use of waste, such as kitchen waste and waste frying oil [14,15].

Among the different orange species, Citrus sinensis is the most widely produced and consumed and as a result, huge amounts of inedible peels are discarded [16]. Also, Citrus limon (lemon), the third most important citrus fruit, is used, to a large extent, for culinary and non-culinary purposes throughout the world. After juice extraction, the peels of both are often discarded. Because of their chemical composition consisting mainly of proteins and fibers and less of oils and antioxidants, they can serve as excellent carbon sources for the synthesis of CQDs [17]. Sahu et al. have described the production of CQDs from the juice of Citrus nobilis deliciosa, which were used for cellular imaging [18]. Tiong et al. produced CQDs from green orange peels (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae Viride) for the non-selective metal ion sensing [19]. Prasannan et al. obtained CQDs from oxidized orange peels, which were modified with ZnO, before used for photocatalytic purposes [20].

In the study herein, we propose the utilization of Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon peels in one-step synthetic procedures via carbonization at 180°C, to produce two kinds of CQDs, thus offering a practicable alternative to the methods so far employed [20]. Although many of the features of the generated CQDs are alike, their distinct analytical properties support their dissimilarity. The strong photoluminescent properties of the two as-prepared CQDs, in combination with the presence of oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups on their surface, make feasible their utilization in various applications. The produced CQDs from Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon can serve, at very small amounts, as selective and highly sensitive fluorescent chemosensors for Fe3+ and tartrazine detection, respectively, in a range of matrices. Furthermore, their excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity over various cell lines, along with their optical merits, strongly reinforce their applicability potential as cell imaging agents and for intracellular detection of Fe3+. The simplicity of the synthesis (no chemicals are needed and little infrastructure is required) combined with the overall low cost of the synthesis (residual resources are utilized as precursors) and the scarcity of relevant methods for tartrazine, highlight the merits of the proposed procedure for CQDs production and applications.
Oranges (Citrus sinensis) and lemons (Citrus limon) were collected from two groves in Epirus, Greece. All information about chemicals and materials is provided in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from orange and lemon peels
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh oranges or lemons were peeled off and their peels were rinsed with double distilled water (DDW). After drying with a paper towel, they were cut into small square pieces (~ 2 × 2cm), placed in separate porcelain crucibles and they were heated for 2h in a pre-heated furnace, at 180°C. After cooling down to room temperature, the homogenous black residues were ground into fine powder, in an achate mortar. The powders were dispersed into 40mL of DDW using ultrasonication, for 10min. The dispersions were centrifuged at 8000rpm, for 5min and the supernatants were filtered through 0.20µm syringe filters (Corning®). The two dispersions were collected, freeze-dried and the light-brown residues obtained were stored in dark, until use.
Details about the instrumentation for sensing, characterization and preparation of samples are given in the Supplementary material.
The QYs of the as-synthesized CQDs were determined by a comparative method (details are given in Supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing approaches for Fe3+ and tartrazine determination
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing procedure for Fe3+ and tartrazine was as follows: 100μL of a working standard solution or a sample solution was added into 900μL of formic acid-sodium formate buffer (10mM, pH 3.0 for Fe3+-Citrus sinensis CQDs) or DDW (for tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs) containing 50μg of CQDs. After vortexing the mixture for 30s, the quenching of the native fluorescence of the CQDs was measured at 365/455nm (λex/λem) for Citrus sinensis CQDs and 330/435nm for Citrus limon CQDs. For the assays, calibration curves were built by plotting the fluorescence quenching - after monitoring the original (F 0) and quenched (F) fluorescence – against Fe3+ or tartrazine concentrations. The selectivities were evaluated by measuring the fluorescence quenching percentages, provoked by other metal ions or potential matrix components. Details about samples and their preparation are given in the Supplementary material.

The cytotoxicities of Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon CQDs were evaluated on four epithelial cell lines (A549, HeLa, MDA-MB-231 and HEK-293) using the crystal violet assay [21]. The HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM medium while the rest of the cell lines were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics. More details about the procedure are provided in the Supplementary material.

The potential bioimaging applicability of the CQDs was evaluated against the four cell lines, mentioned above. To this end, cells were seeded on 22 × 24mm glass cover-slips, placed inside a 6-well plate and they were incubated for 5h. One mL of fresh medium containing CQDs, at a concentration of 200μg/mL, was added to each well and the cells were incubated for 2h. Then, the cover slips were washed three times with PBS and kept in there, until observation. The cells on the cover slips were examined with fluorescence microscopy, under UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular detection of Fe3+ in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were treated with Citrus sinensis CQDs following the procedure mentioned above. After 2h of incubation with the CQDs, the cells were washed with PBS and 2mL of fresh DMEM medium, containing 100μM exogenous Fe3+ was added to half of them, while 2mL of DMEM medium (iron-free) was added to the rest [22]. After 6h of incubation, the cells were examined on a fluorescence microscope, under UV light.

The as-prepared CQDs were able to uniformly disperse without apparent aggregation. The CQDs have circular shapes, diameters of ca. 6.5 and 4.5nm for Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon, respectively and narrow size distributions (Fig. S1 of Supplementary material). The FTIR spectra of the two as-synthesized CQDs (Fig. S2 of Supplementary material) are pretty much alike exhibiting a broad absorption band around 3300cm−1, which is characteristic of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations and suggests the presence of the corresponding functional groups. The absorption bands at 1718, 1369 and 1230cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching of –C˭O (carbonyl), bending of –OH and stretching of –C–O (acid) groups, respectively [23]. The peaks at 1600 and 1405cm−1 are due to the C˭C stretching and –COO- asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations [20,23]. The stretching vibrations of sp 2 and sp 3 C–H are visible near 2930 and 2850cm−1, respectively.

XRD analysis (Fig. S3 of Supplementary material) was carried out to assess the crystallinity of CQDs. The pattern shows an obvious broad peak at around 2θ = 23°, which indicates the amorphous nature of dots [24]. The interlayer spacings of Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon CQDs were calculated to be 0.35 and 0.37nm, respectively, which are close to or slightly higher than that of graphite (0.34nm).

An XPS study was also performed for the characterization of CQDs. Fig. 1 shows the detailed core level peaks of a combined C1s-K2p window, of O1s and N1s. The C1s peak is analyzed into five components (C1-C5) assigned to C–C sp 2 and defective sp 3 bonds, at binding energies of 284.7eV and 285.6eV, respectively, to carbon–oxygen components, both hydroxyl and epoxy, at 286.4eV, to carbonyls at 288.3eV and to a small contribution of carboxyls, at 291.5eV. In the same window, the K2p peak at binding energy of K2p3/2 at 292.8eV is assigned to K–O bonds [25]. The deconvoluted O1s peak consists of two components assigned to carbon-oxygen double bonds (531.1eV), single bonds (532.6eV) and of a third low-intensity component (~ 534eV), which is assigned to adsorbed H2O [26]. A minor contribution of sample holder is present in both C1s and O1s peaks. Finally, the N1s peak is deconvoluted into two components and the binding energies of 399.2eV and 400.1eV are assigned to pyridinic N surface groups (N˭C(H)) and amine/amide or pyrolic moieties (N-C(H)), respectively [27].

The UV–vis spectra of the synthesized CQDs are almost identical (Fig. S4 of Supplementary material) and exhibit a broad absorption band at ~ 270nm, which can be attributed to the n-π* and π-π* transition of the –C˭O bonds of carboxyl groups and aromatic sp 2 (–C˭C–) domains, respectively, in accordance with earlier reports [28,29]. Both as-synthesized CQDs are highly hydrophilic and, therefore, highly dispersed solutions can be prepared, which are transparent, yellow-colored. Aqueous dispersions of CQDs irradiated under UV light, exhibit bright blue luminescence (insets of Fig. S4 of Supplementary material).

The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths. From Fig. 2a, the excitation-fluorescence behavior shows narrow and symmetrical fluorescence spectra for Citrus sinensis CQDs with a strong emission band, peaking at 455nm, when excited at 365nm. The excitation profile monitored at an emission of 455nm, (Fig. S5 of Supplementary material) indicates that the maximum fluorescence emission of the synthesized Citrus sinensis CQDs is achieved at an excitation wavelength of 365nm. The maxima of excitation and emission spectra of the synthesized Citrus sinensis CQDs differ from previously synthesized CQDs deriving from other orange peels species, as a shift towards higher wavelengths of approximately 20–25nm is noticed [19,20].

The fluorescence spectra of Citrus limon CQDs (Fig. 2b) exhibit two emission peaks at 390nm (violet) and 435nm (blue), at all excitation wavelengths set, differentiating them from the Citrus sinensis CQDs. Pyrolysis at a certain temperature can lead to the formation of molecular fluorophores and carbogenic cores with different quantum yield [30]. In this dual emissive mode of Citrus sinensis CQDs, it is apparent that both forms exist, at different ratios. For both peaks, the strongest fluorescence intensity is associated with an excitation wavelength of 330nm (Fig. S5 of Supplementary material). It is noteworthy that the recorded emissions of the two kinds of CQDs are excitation-independent, bespeaking the uniformity of CQDs surface states (same emissive sites for each kind of dots) and size. The QYs of the CQDs in the respective excitation-emission pairs were calculated to be 16.8% and 15.5% for Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon respectively, using quinine sulphate as reference. These yields are higher than those of CQDs obtained by other green syntheses [31,32].

The photoluminescence of both kinds of CQDs is constant in the pH range of 3.0–10.0. In more acidic ambience (pH<3.0) and even pronouncedly in alkaline conditions (pH>10.0), the original fluorescence intensity of Citrus sinensis CQDs is dramatically decreased, in contrast to Citrus limon analogues, which exhibit almost invariable emission up to pH 12.0. As a result, the CQDs can be employed in sensing applications, in a wide range of pH (Fig. S6 of Supplementary material). It seems that the changeable pH values can lead to variation in the deprotonation degree of surface atoms of dots and consequently, in alterations in their fluorescence intensity [33]. Moreover, both kinds of CQDs possess excellent photoluminescence stability in solutions with varying ionic strength, at concentrations of NaCl and Na2SO4 up to 1.0molL−1 (Fig. S7 of Supplementary material).

In other experiments, the fluorescence of CQDs solutions, continuously irradiated for 2h with a 150W Xe lamp was stable, suggesting satisfactory photostability for both as-prepared CQDs. Finally, the fluorescence of CQDs solutions, stored at room temperature, scarcely diminished with time (<5% and<2%, for Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon CQDs, respectively, after 72h of storage). Likewise, solutions prepared from lyophilized dots and stored for more than six months were almost unabated. The fluorescence quenching was swift for both kinds of CQDs and stable over a time period of more than 20min. All the above data, hint at a favorable photostability of the synthesized CQDs, which renders them promising for applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for Fe3+ and tartrazine sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Because, mainly, of the presence of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on their surfaces, the as-prepared CQDs could serve as potential fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions. From the experiments conducted, it becomes evident that Fe3+ quenches the natively emitted fluorescence of Citrus sinensis CQDs (100μM of Fe3+ causes a 60% decline in the original emission intensity), corroborating the aforementioned hypothesis.

Citrus limon CQDs were also tested for their potential to be used as a fluorescent probe. Pertinent experiments revealed that 25μM of tartrazine was able to satisfactory quench the emission of the CQDs by almost 60% while the fluorescence is affected much less by metal ions. As a result, the Citrus limon CQDs were utilized to develop a method for the detection of tartrazine (Fig. S8 of Supplementary material).

To maximize the detection potential of the CQDs as probes for Fe3+ and tartrazine, experimental conditions were optimized taking into consideration the outcome of the stability study. Since the CQDs were found to be stable in the pH ranges of 3.0–10.0 and 3.0–12.0, optimum working pH for the chemosensors was examined within these ranges. A 10% increase in fluorescence quenching of Fe3+- Citrus sinensis CQDs occurs, when the pH of the solution is maintained at 3.0 (data not shown). This pH value, at low concentrations of Fe3+ ensures that hydrolysis of iron does not take place [34]. To widen the applicability of the proposed fluorescent probe towards various matrices, pH variations shall be the minimum. In this context, formic acid-sodium formate buffer at pH 3.0 was found to be suitable for sensing purposes since it maximizes the fluorescence quenching. Finally, the quenching of the fluorescence of tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs system was unaffected by the pH over the studied range. This negates the need for pH adjustment, simplifying the overall method and reducing the variations which may be caused by the matrix pH.

The synthesized Citrus sinensis CQDs were used as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ in biological and environmental matrices. Prior to testing their applicability, it was fundamental to ensure their selectivity towards Fe3+. In this regard, the quenching of CQDs fluorescence emission caused by other metal ions was examined, at concentrations of 100μM, under the optimum conditions for Fe3+ sensing. (The selectivity of the CQDs towards Fe3+ is obvious, as it can be seen from Fig. S9 of Supplementary material). Other than Hg2+ and secondarily Pb2+ which cause a fluorescence quenching of up to 20% and 12%, respectively, the rest of metal ions are not able to quench the fluorescence of the CQDs, to a considerable extent. However, the interference from Hg2+ and Pb2+ is of little concern, since their concentrations in real-life environmental and biological samples are quite lower than that of Fe3+. Tartrazine, the basic quencher of Citrus limon CQDs, causes only 5% quenching.

Fig. 3 depicts, graphically, the fall in fluorescence intensity (F 0/F), versus Fe3+, at concentrations in the range of 0.01–400μM, at 25°C. The Stern-Volmer plot for the quenching is non-linear and is thought to be a composite of two linear plots derived from two fluorophore species, one accessible and the other partially accessible to quencher [35]. It can be seen that the decrease in fluorescence intensity is in linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+, in the low range of 0.01μM to 1.0μM (inset of Fig. 3), with a coefficient of determination (R 2) of 0.998. The limit of detection of the sensing method – calculated as signal-to-noise ratio = 3 – was estimated to be 3.0nM and the relative standard deviation (% RSD) for three measurements of 1.0μM Fe3+ was 2.5% for intra-day (reproducibility) and 3.2% for inter-day (repeatability) assays.

Lastly, the analytical applicability of the developed sensing approach was demonstrated by analyzing environmental water samples, human blood and urine and by performing recovery experiments (Table S1 of Supplementary material).

Satisfactory recoveries were obtained in the range of 94.0–99.0% and 94.0–98.0% for Fe3+ in human blood and urine samples respectively, while recoveries from the river and sea water samples were 97.0% and 98.0%. Therefore, the matrix effects are absent, alluding to the applicability potential of method for the detection of Fe3+ in relevant matrixes.

Tartrazine is a kind of water-soluble synthetic azo-colorant and one of the most controversial food additives, since studies have revealed that it may cause adverse health effects [36–38]. The proposed method is highly selective towards tartrazine (Fig. S9 of Supplementary material), since 25.0μM of the colorant cause ~ 60% reduction of the emitted fluorescence, whereas the same concentrations of other compounds commonly found in foodstuffs and certain metal ions do not cause significant quenching (<5% in all cases examined).

Fig. 4 portrays the fall in emission intensity upon adding tartrazine up to a concentration of 80.0μM, at 25°C. The quenching caused by tartrazine was found to be linear between 0.6μM and 23.5μΜ (R 2 = 0.9936) (inset of Fig. 4). The limit of detection was 0.2μM and the RSD (%) for three measurements of 23.0μM tartrazine was calculated to be 2.3% for intra-day (reproducibility) and 3.4% for inter-day (repeatability) assays. The analytical applicability of the method was proven by analyzing food products (ice-cream, juice and energy drink) and by recovery experiments (Table S2 of Supplementary material). Highly satisfactory recoveries were achieved, ranging between 95.0% and 99.9%, which confirm that the probe is interference-free and underline its applicability.

From the results presented herein, the analytical merits of the developed methods for Fe3+ and tartrazine are rather obvious. The proposed analytical probes for Fe3+ and tartrazine sensing from citrus species can be used for environmental, biological and food analysis and the recoveries, LODs and linear ranges achieved rival those of other developed analytical probes or are even better, as presented in Table S3 of Supplementary material.

In principle, there are several potential causes of quenching, such as the inner-filter effect, the dynamic quenching and the static quenching [35]. The quenching behavior of Fe3+ and tartrazine on the fluorescence of Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon CQDs respectively, can be illustrated by the well-known Stern-Volmer Eq. (1):
(1)
F
0
F
=
1 +
K
SV
[
Q
]
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher (Fe3+ or tartrazine), respectively, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher and K
SV is the Stern-Voler constant, which defines the quenching efficiency of the quencher. The plots of F 0/F as a function of Fe3+ (Citrus sinensis CQDs) and tartrazine (Citrus limon CQDs), at three different temperatures (25, 35 and 45°C) and concentrations below 1.0μM and 23.5μM for Fe3+ and tartrazine, respectively, show linear relationships (Fig. S10 of Supplementary material). The slopes of plots correlate inversely with temperature hinting at static quenching mechanisms [39]. Increased temperatures are likely to result in decreased complex stability and thus, the static quenching constants (i.e. Stern-Voler constants) are expected to decrease. At high concentrations of quenchers, the Stern-Volmer plots become highly non-linear. The data show that the extent of quenching of Citrus sinensis CQDs decreases by increasing the concentration of Fe3+. By contrast, the upward curvature of the Stern-Volmer plot for tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs, concave towards the y-axis, entails that the involvement of a combined mechanism (dynamic and static) cannot be ruled out [35].

To gain further insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the molecular absorption behavior of Fe3+, tartrazine and the respective CQDs were studied. Fig. 5 demonstrates a subtle overlapping between the excitation of Citrus sinensis CQDs and absorption of Fe3+, while tartrazine absorption and emission of Citrus limon CQDs significantly overlap. In addition, the absorption spectra of quencher (Fe3+ or tartrazine)-CQDs, which coincide with the superposition of spectra of quencher and CQDs separately, exhibit increased absorption with an increase in quencher concentration, at critical wavelengths. This suggests that the fluorescence quenching induced by Fe3+ and even more by tartrazine, may arise, albeit to a variable degree, from the inner filter effect (Fig. S11 of Supplementary material).

The mechanism of inner filter effect is different from quenching (dynamic or static), although the expected result is similar and absolutely exploitable for analytical purposes [40]. Since the absorption increases with the increase in the quencher concentration, the apparent quenching induced, increases the real magnitude of the quenching constants obtained from steady state experiments. To correct for the inner filter effect, the following general formula (2) was employed (the collected intensity comes exactly from the center of the cuvette) to investigate the remaining quenching effect
(2)
F corr
=
F obs
×
10
(
A
e x
+
A em
) / 2
where F
corr is the corrected fluorescence value, F
obs the measured fluorescence value and A ex and A em are the absorption values at the excitation and emission wavelength, respectively. The fitted linear Stern-Volmer plot (Eq. (1)) between the net quenching efficiency of the CQDs and the concentrations of Fe3+ and tartrazine (Fig. S12 of Supplementary material) provided the K SV values of the systems, which are 2.0 × 106 M−1 and 7.1 × 103 M−1 for the Fe3+-Citrus sinensis CQDs and tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs, respectively. The upward curvature of the Stern-Volmer plot for tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs does not appear anymore and hence, the static quenching mechanism is operative. For this mechanism, the binding constant (K b) and the Hill coefficient (n) can be determined, as follows:
(3)
log
F
0
−
F
F
=
log
Κ
b
+
nlog
[
Q
]
where F 0 and F and are the same as in Eq. (1) and [Q] is the free concentration of the quencher. At low quencher concentrations, where deviations between free and added concentrations are the highest, the influence on the linear fit is predicted to be high. Moreover, since the K b is the power of 10 of the intercept, it may be extremely sensitive to small changes in the slope of the fitting procedure [41]. To avoid these pitfalls for the low-range concentrations of Fe3+, only the K b value of tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs system was calculated, at 25°C and it was found to be 8.4 × 103 M−1 (Fig. S13 of Supplementary material), which is very close to that calculated by Eq. (1). The calculated constants indicate that there is strong binding between Fe3+ and CQDs and less intensive between tartrazine and CQDs. The n value for tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs was close to the unit (1.02), indicating the occurrence of just one high affinity-binding site on CQDs for tartrazine.

Additional experiments were undertaken using EDTA as competitive chelating agent to bind to Fe3+ and recover the quenched photoluminescence of the Fe3+-Citrus sinensis CQDs system. They showed that EDTA was unable to act in this way, even at concentrations fifteen times higher than Fe3+, which might be attributed to the intensive chelation of Fe3+ on the surface of CQDs. Also, tartrazine, as an anionic dye mimics, somehow, sodium dodecyl sulphate because both molecules have a sulphate group at one end [42]. Added concentrations, fifteen times higher than tartrazine, caused no detectable quenching effect, thus excluding the involvement of sulfonate group in the quenching mechanism. Furthermore, a slight blue shift of about 6nm noticed in the λem of tartrazine-Citrus limon CQDs system – which is not obvious in Fe3+-Citrus sinensis CQDs system – (Fig. S8) implies that the fluorophores of Citrus sinensis CQDs are placed in a more hydrophobic microenvironment, in the presence of tartrazine [43].

XPS is a sensitive tool to analyze the atomic composition of the surface of CQDs and can account for differences between CQDs. From the total intensities of C1s, O1s, N1s and K2p peaks, the relative atomic compositions of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and potassium were calculated and are tabulated in Table 1. Between the two kinds of CQDs, small differences are detected in terms of % C˭O and % C–O bonds. The defective sp 3 bonds, which are present in Citrus limon CQDs are negligible in Citrus sinensis CQDs. The percentage of C˭O bonds is similar in both CQDs. More striking is the difference of pyridinic N (N˭C(H)) fraction between Citrus sinensis CQDs and Citrus limon CQDs, which is higher in the former dots, while the opposite holds true for amine/amide or pyrolic N (N–C(H)).

Based on the above data and reasoning, the fluorescence quenching due to Fe3+ may be safely attributed to a particular type of co-ordination interaction with Citrus sinensis CQDs structure, which results in the formation of stable complex with oxygen- and electron-rich pyridinic-nitrogen containing groups. Based on the above inference, the type and location of functional groups may be important. As regards the quenching of Citrus limon CQDs because of the presence of tartrazine, the hydrophobic microenvironment entails hydrophobic interactions. In addition, the hydrogen bonds, due to plenty of amenable groups in quencher and fluorophore, are anticipated to play major role in the formation of tartrazine-Citrus sinensis CQDs complex.

The CQDs exhibit extremely low cytotoxicity, at all concentrations tested (Fig. S14 of Supplementary material). It is noteworthy that the cell viability, after 48h of incubation with 400μg/mL of Citrus sinensis CQDs and Citrus limon CQDs is above 90% and 95%, respectively, for all cell lines. The results of the toxicity assay combined with the aforementioned optical merits, signify that the synthesized CQDs are promising candidates for in vivo imaging or other applications, such as cellular labeling, optical barcoding, medical imaging [44]. Fig. 6 shows cells treated with Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon CQDs (All cell lines examined can be seen in details, in Figs. S15 and S16 of Supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular detection of Fe3+ in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of Citrus sinensis CQDs in detecting intracellular Fe3+ was evaluated with the addition of exogenous Fe3+ in HeLa cells, treated previously with Citrus sinensis CQDs. From Fig. S17 of Supplementary material, it is seen that the non-treated cells show almost no background fluorescence, while the treated cells are lightened, as a result of CQDs permeation through cell membrane (probably through passive endocytosis [45]), signifying that the CQDs are located in the cytoplasmic area and making feasible intracellular biosensing. Moreover, it can be seen that the intercellular fluorescence of cells, supplemented with Fe3+ is very weak, compared to the control cells. This highlights the promising potential of Citrus sinensis CQDs to be employed as probe for monitoring Fe3+ in living cells.

The recouping of residual resources via carbonization to quantum dots has the added benefit of reclaiming a waste that might otherwise simply find its way into landfills. In conclusion, Citrus sinensis and Citrus limon peels can serve as precursors for the green production of two kinds of highly fluorescent CQDs, via an unsophisticated, easy and low-cost method, that is consistent with the principles of green chemistry. The as-synthesized CQDs possess optical merits and favorable stability, under various storage and working conditions. Although many of their features are alike, our findings demonstrate that each kind of the generated CQDs lend itself to quite distinct analytical applications. The CQDs were used as fluorescent probes for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions in human blood, urine and water samples and tartrazine in foodstuffs. This sensing approach is characterized by the rapidity of analysis, simplicity (sequential addition of components to assay solution), high reproducibility and absence of interferences. Moreover, the synthesized CQDs possess excellent biocompatibility and are employed for cell imaging and intracellular detection of Fe3+ in cells. The quenching mechanisms, most likely, fall in a regime of static quenching, in addition to inner filter effect.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Many thanks are due to the XRD center of University of Ioannina for obtaining the XRD spectra.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.07.053.
